pattern: The winning pair always has one foot at either end of the row, since anything a middle house could achieve against its partner is matched or beaten by pairing that same partner with whichever end shares its own color.
hints: You never need to compare every pair -- the best possible distance always involves house 0 or the very last house.
For each house, check whether it differs from house 0 and, separately, whether it differs from the last house, recording the farther distance either comparison allows.
Take the single largest distance found across both of those anchor comparisons; that is the answer, no matter which end it came from.

edge_inputs.description: one lone differently-colored house near the middle of a uniform row
alternating colors across a short row
the smallest possible row, two houses
the two ends share a color but a middle house differs from both, well past either end alone
only the very first house differs from an otherwise uniform row
every house is a distinct color
the two ends already differ from each other, so no middle house can beat that span

stress.description: random rows near the length cap, forced to keep at least two distinct colors
